headline: Yes — the two JS scripts can be folded in, but the natural home is a new ECR-scan submodule that sits alongside CloudSploitToolkit and reuses ECRToolkit's existing aioboto3 call, NOT the CloudSploit CLI executor.

localization.role: Add agent-facing public method(s): collect_ecr_findings(...) and generate_ecr_report(...). Holds the new EcrScanCollector + EcrReportGenerator collaborators.
Independent Pydantic models for ECR Basic Scanning shape — distinct from SeverityLevel(OK/WARN/FAIL/UNKNOWN).
Implements the multi-repo / tag-priority loop with bounded concurrency. Composes AWSInterface (aioboto3) to call describe_image_scan_findings — does NOT shell out to CloudSploit.
New method that renders the interactive HTML report from EcrCollectionResult. Browser-targeted (not PDF).
Jinja2 port of the JS generator's self-contained HTML/CSS/JS report.

Existing per-image wrapper. Likely needs an opt-in flag (e.g. include_attributes=True) so attributes[] (package_name, package_version, fixed_in_versions, CVSS) survives normalization for downstream report rendering.
Reusable persistence path; ECR collection calls it with scanner='ecr-image-scan' (or similar) — no schema changes required for v1.

constraints.statement: Auth must flow through AWSInterface (aioboto3, navconfig AWS_CREDENTIALS), not by reading ~/.cloudsploit/aws/credentials.json directly.
ECR Basic Scanning severities (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/INFORMATIONAL) are incompatible with the toolkit's existing SeverityLevel enum (OK/WARN/FAIL/UNKNOWN). A new enum is required.
All public toolkit methods must be async — the file is wired around AbstractToolkit which auto-exposes async methods as agent tools.

The HTML report is browser-targeted (interactive filters, gradients, grid layout) and is not safe to feed into xhtml2pdf without a separate print-CSS template. Either skip PDF for ECR v1, or produce a parallel print template.
Multi-repo iteration should use bounded concurrency (e.g. asyncio.Semaphore(5)) — the JS script is strictly sequential and the Python port can do better; raw fan-out would hit ECR rate limits.
The repo+tag list must NOT be hard-coded — it's currently 23 entries with custom tag priorities and will drift. Use a Pydantic-validated plan (YAML or JSON) loaded at scan time.

recent_history.summary: FEAT-160 added --config plumbing to CloudSploitToolkit (commits f34c33d6...bfb825e7); the _resolve_config pattern (toolkit.py:75-101) is the template to follow for per-call overrides on the new ECR methods.

InspectorToolkit (commits 5d88b83c...0a46bcd6) covers ECR via Inspector v2 / Enhanced Scanning with richer normalization (vulnerable_packages with fixed_in_version, package_manager, file_path). ECR Basic Scanning is the legacy path the JS script targets; we should keep both supported.
security-report-catalog (TASK-1110) wired CloudSploitToolkit through ReportPersistenceMixin — ECR collection should opt into the same mixin (no schema change needed).
Branch tip has 'wip: aws agent' (8b7eb250) — coordinate with that work so the new ECR scan tools are reachable from the AWS agent.

hypotheses.statement: Add an EcrScanCollector + EcrCollectionPlan + EcrReportGenerator triple inside cloudsploit/, wire two new async public methods on CloudSploitToolkit (collect_ecr_findings, generate_ecr_report), reusing ECRToolkit's existing aioboto3 call (after extending it to keep attributes).
hypotheses.rationale: Maps every script behavior to an existing pattern in the repo: aioboto3 via AWSInterface (F003), Jinja2 reports (F004), persistence mixin (F005). The only net-new code is the per-repo/per-tag loop and an HTML template.
hypotheses.scope: Single feature branch; net-new files: ecr_collector.py, ecr_scan_report.html. Modified: models.py (+5 classes), toolkit.py (+2 methods, +1 collaborator instance), reports.py (+2 methods), aws/ecr.py (extend wrapper). One new test module mirroring tests/cloudsploit/test_*.py.

hypotheses.statement: Alternative: graft the ECR scan loop onto ECRToolkit (aws/ecr.py) instead of CloudSploitToolkit. The user framed it as part of 'our CloudSploit toolkit', so this is not the recommended placement but is mentioned as an option.
hypotheses.rationale: ECRToolkit is the natural home from a domain-modeling standpoint (it talks to the ECR API). But the user explicitly mentions the CloudSploit toolkit and wants the report aesthetic alongside CSPM reports. Splitting collection + reporting across two toolkits adds coupling.
hypotheses.scope: Reduced surface in cloudsploit/, expanded surface in aws/ — tradeoff is worse for the user's stated framing.

hypotheses.statement: Stretch: also wire the InspectorToolkit-based Enhanced Scanning path as a second source so repos with Enhanced Scanning enabled produce richer per-package data. The collector picks per-repo whichever data source is available.
hypotheses.rationale: Inspector v2 normalization already extracts package data the JS script struggles to get from Basic Scanning's attributes[] array. Adding it gives a clean upgrade path. But it expands scope; reasonable to defer to a follow-up.
hypotheses.scope: Additional collector strategy file. Worth a separate FEAT if user is interested.

confidence_map.claim: aws_ecr_get_image_scan_findings already exists and is the right call to wrap
CloudSploitToolkit is auto-exposed via AbstractToolkit, so new async methods become agent tools for free
Severity model needs a new enum independent from SeverityLevel(OK/WARN/FAIL/UNKNOWN)
Jinja2 + xhtml2pdf is the canonical reporting pattern
ReportPersistenceMixin will accept ECR scans with no schema change in v1
Existing ECR wrapper drops the attributes[] payload — needs extension to keep package/CVSS/fixed-in
Bounded concurrency (semaphore) is correct over both raw fan-out and strict sequential
Repo+tag list should be config-driven (YAML/JSON), not hard-coded
Browser-targeted HTML report cannot reuse xhtml2pdf for PDF — needs a separate print template if PDF is desired

unknowns.question: Where should the repo+tag list live?
unknowns.user_answer: YAML cargado en runtime (Pydantic-validated, path passed to collect_ecr_findings(plan=...)).
plausible_answers: YAML file path passed to collect_ecr_findings(plan=...) — best for ops
Hard-coded in a config module under parrot_tools/cloudsploit/ — easy but drifts
Argument from the agent at runtime — flexible but loses the curated default

unknowns.question: Support Basic + Enhanced in v1 or Basic-only?
unknowns.user_answer: Basic only in v1. Enhanced/Inspector v2 deferred to a follow-up FEAT.
plausible_answers: Basic only in v1, Enhanced as FEAT-XXX follow-up — faster to ship
Both in v1 with a per-repo strategy selector — richer data day-one
Enhanced only via InspectorToolkit — drop Basic Scanning support

unknowns.question: PDF variant of the ECR HTML report?
unknowns.user_answer: HTML only — the interactive features are the point. No PDF in v1.
plausible_answers: HTML only — the interactive features are the point
HTML + simplified print template for PDF — keeps parity with the rest of the toolkit
Both shipped together, with the print template being the user's responsibility to style

unknowns.question: Toolkit home for the new public methods?
unknowns.user_answer: CloudSploitToolkit — keeps security-posture and vuln scans grouped together (user's framing).
plausible_answers: Both — thin facade on each that delegates to a shared collector

recommended_next_command.rationale: Confidence is high on localization and feasibility (every JS behavior maps cleanly to existing patterns). Four open questions are scope decisions, not feasibility risks — they can be answered in the spec discussion. There is no need to explore architectural alternatives via brainstorm.

alternatives.when: if you want to explicitly score H-1 vs H-2 vs H-3 with library/code references before specing
